comments.author: Steve
comments.text: It's possible that we can fix one part of the proximal problem simply by changing the internals of tryHandMergedMerged. But I am still reading.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I am starting to think that this code can just be retired, actually. Our seven tricks should be sufficient enough in their own right.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: This is clearly out of scope for the hints system, at least on the player side. The player should only get hints when they can't play a card out of their hand.  This move is a scoring optimization, so it may have value in the future, but it just muddies the water now.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: This trick is the true-beginner trick. We will want beginners to see one card and once card only that can get on to the board. So direct_play is our "favorite" trick.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Ah, this is the place where we want to prioritize pure runs first.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yay! We have more code to eliminate! Your design changes to the wire format make this reverse-engineering unnecessary.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: The shape of this is excellent. Just to be clear, we aren't going to eliminate info to the client. We are going to change the ranking priority. The ranking priority will NOT be a metric. We will make it follow out simply by the order in which we process the tricks.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Ah! Now I spot my confusion. The 6 cards that showed to me in my "bad" hint really WERE just one hint. That part wasn't broken. It was a very unusual case of having a valid 6-card red-black run in my hand.  I was quote-unquote lucky!

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Fortunately, computational cost will always be a non-issue at Lym Rummy scale. This doesn't mean we should be deliberately wasteful haha.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: That's fine. They kinda have to.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: I think that's fine. For our use cases, I actually believe that's a feature, not a flaw, of the system. But we do need to make sure our assumptions are explicit.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: We won't fix that immediately.  It might actually be the right pattern, but it does small like needless complexity the way you phrase it.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: Yeah, that's yucky but maybe hard to fix.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: We will establish this pattern. Info is cheap to pass over the wire. It's not a sin to tell a client more than it actually needs. It's the client's job (and option) to use the data however it wants.

comments.author: Steve
comments.text: yup, that's where the requirements change today
